Meaning to convey:
Convey this: Chains of interconnectivity, associative relationships, axes in trees in forests with birds and hunters in a tundra, fighting to survive. Words cluster into one hierarchical dimension, one Russian doll of meaning, the tangible reality of the forest. Or trees of ancestry are studded with gems glinting in the past, smiling within their distant constellations. A poetic dimension emerges, borrowing from trees the visual attribute of interconnected roots, hence the proverbial ancestral tree.

Edit: In the aircraft cockpit, a pilot is surrounded by an array of controls, including toggles, switches, and gauges that provide real-time data on crucial parameters such as altitude,

airspeed, and engine thrust. This information serves as a comprehensive communication system, metaphorically conveying the aircraft’s status to the pilot. The pilot can then employ gestures, manipulating controls like the throttle, to express directional commands such as turning left, right, climbing, or descending. In response, the aircraft executes these commands, prompting the pilot to evaluate the maneuver, thereby sustaining an ongoing and dynamic interaction between the pilot and the aircraft.

Meaning to convey:
Convey this: Prior models, such as recurrent and convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures struggled to capture relationships and dependencies between words essential to contextual understanding. The Transformer self attention mechanism allows AI to digest entire sentences in one fell swoop, a stark departure from the methodical, step-by-step parsing of the past. This breakthrough set the stage for an era where AI’s grasp of language mirrors the nuanced understanding of its human creators.

Meaning to convey:
Convey this: The challenge for me as a rhetorician and user experience designer, will be to offer a perspective ultimately at a conceptual distance from the actual code. Most developers with PHds in AI are furiously taking notes during job interviews for Lead designer on AI teams. In fact I’ve been on AI teams so big that they contain over a dozen engineers qualified to the gills with doctorates and masters degrees in artificial intelligence and machine learning, and on one such occasion my suggestion to explore hyoernyms and about a dozen other semantic relationship types in their pipelines, leading to a breakthrough after months of grief, and suddenly the AI responses were hitting birdies. I was still laid off though, so put on your AI entrepreneurial helmet, those are coming.

The word “context” finds its roots in the Latin language, deriving from “con-”

(meaning “together” or “with”) and the verb “texere” (meaning “to weave” or “to construct”). It follows that context is like a quilt, with an interwoven construction pulling many different ideas together “with” each other to produce a meaning greater than the sum of its parts.

Context of the Quilt Itself (Sentences or Entire Story): Just as words come together to form sentences or a story, these patches combine to form the complete image—the quilt’s full design.

This level of interpretation looks at how the patches (words) interact, what relationships exist between them, and how they contribute to the overall image (sentence or story). It’s this synthesis of all the patches that allows us to understand what the quilt depicts, whether it’s a landscape, a portrait, or an abstract design.

I like to think of all of this, particularly the way AI ‘thinks’, as a quilt, with atomically constructed layers of meaning. Layer upon layer, until the entire picture is understood

as a rich context. In the quilt have individual threads made up of distinct vibrant heus, come together to create the patches in the quilt as the first level of meaning. From there, the second layer of meaning combines quilt patches create distinct pictures of let’s say a person sitting, a table with coffee on it and a laptop, a bakery counter with baristas, the third layer of meaning is the rich context that combines these elements into a writer working in a bustling cafe, and the fourth layer of meaning is outside of the picture, with the warmth and utility of the quilt that someone chooses out of a basket to sit by the fireside.

Patterns compound atomically. Higher order patterns are comprised of multiple smaller order patterns, just as multiple atoms comprise a molecule, forming meta-patterns. Pattern-ception, patterns within patterns within patterns. Leonardo DiCaprio. Everyone knows the major scale as Do Re Mi. It’s do-re-mi-fa-so-la-ti-do. In the music interval known as the perfect fourth, comprised of

do and fa, unfolds a profound sonic signature, a tonal symmetry that resonates with wonder at the sight of an ancient celestial archway. On the other hand the dissonance of a major 7th, do and te, exudes a spacey and ethereal quality as it’s tinged with longing for resolution, but not quite there. Speaking of Christopher Nolan movies, you might hear the major 7th in the score for Interstellar at the edge of the universe. These sonic signatures are higher level patterns, and while they themselves are comprised of the lower-level patterns of individual tones, their poetic and emotional affect becomes the sonic palette with which composers create musical sentences, which themselves become yet even higher order patterns. Patterns build atomically and are nested.

In essence what users are doing here is partaking in a bit of programming themselves. Thag’s fundamenrally what ChatGPT users are doing. The creation of structures is inevitable in prompt creation. As we said, meaning is connected at an existential level to order, as words find

root in the ordering force of pattern recognition as the engine of sentient intelligence. A piano is a static program. I may be writing music in my head and I merely have to look at the piano and a whole slew of new sonic possibilities hatch in my mind, because the piano as a program has very unique compositional ambixterous possibilities. But it is also limited to those possibilities by its own static nature. So now we are saying, provide a neural-visual-tactile-sonic interface for me that mirrors a piano, but I want to be able to bend notes. Now we are expanding the programs capabilities — the user is dynamically modifying the structure of the system to suit their current need.

There might be a handful of key outcomes that your users need to accomplish. On the Airblox RFP tool, freight forwarders needed the ability to create packages, complex orders optimized for revenue management across multiple carriers and shipping lanes. Underneath the prompt bar I

put a little chip that says create package. Suddenly the UI changes above, instead of itemizing the conversation linearly, I simply begin the “create package” outcome flow. Here UI meets intelligence in a pre-configured generative UI as the shipping lanes that comprise the package appear, with filters allowing them to sort, make selections. If create package were a prompt, the obvious question an AI agent would ask would be, what lanes should go in this package. The UI infers this natural follow up question with a command-based interface that looks like this.

A semantic domain is a multidimensional space, a complex tapestry woven from strands of meaning that can take many forms - mathematical symbols, musical notes, visual

patterns, even the subtle shades of human emotion. Each domain has its own unique set of elements, its own “color” that imbues it with distinctive meaning. And within each domain, these elements combine and recombine in myriad ways, giving rise to the patterns that form the very foundation of semantics.

We might say, the sensorium of the human and a vampire bat are dramatically different.

Here in the Fireflies UI you can see that every component has the ability to be augmented with AI, with a few subtle shortcomings. The entire UI is generative in terms

of the data, but also in terms of the UI itself. For instance, now users can determine which filters they want. Fireflies is attempting to do this with topic trackers, which are user created filters. Search should be an AI search, not a keyword search. The summary can be tailored to user preferences, articulated through prompts or pre-created design system. Everything can be molded.

Trained on vasts amounts of text data, GPTs or Generative Pretrained Transformers, are specific implementations of LLMs. The keywords in the names are telling signs of the functionality. Multi-modality, such as text-to-speech, or text-to-image are prime examples of the collapse of topical barriers noted above, as LLMs inherit from language the power

of semantic fluidity. In the context of “Generative Pre-trained Transformer,” “Generative” refers to the model’s ability to generate text, “Pre-trained” indicates that the model is initially trained on a large corpus of text data to learn general language patterns, and “Transformer” signifies the underlying architecture used for processing and generating text. So, “Transformer”, the T in GPT specifically points to the architecture that powers the model’s capabilities in understanding and generating human-like text, whereas G describes the underlying execution mechanism being one that is generative in nature which is accomplished via perplexity algorithms that we can touch on later.

So you can imagine the pattern of a sound in this semantic space taking a certain shape. Let’s take the roaring sound of a fountain. It has a silibance, like the letter S, but also

a low rumbling. It is ambient, and constant. The sound of cars going by by contrast are also constant and ambient, but they have a characteristic phase doppler effect as they move, and each car has a unique pitch and speed, all of which allows the whooshing sound of cars to be a distinct pattern from the roaring sound of the fountain. They are similar, but they are not the same. If they were the same, they would be indistinguishable in the semantic vector space, occupying the same space form. But because they are merely similar, the sound of the fountain can be said to be a certain “distance of similarity” from the sound of the cars. The distance in this case is not that long, because they are nearly identical. If they were identical the distance would be zero.

As we develop ever more sophisticated AI systems to understand and interact with the world, it is crucial that we broaden our conception of semantics to include the full

richness and diversity of semantic domains beyond natural language. For it is only by grasping the manifold ways that elements can combine into meaningful patterns that we will create AI that can truly comprehend context and potentiality across all the domains that humans find semantically significant.
